key_inquiry_points: Involuntary leakage synchronous with coughing, sneezing, laughing, or physical exertion, with no preceding urge.
Common after vaginal delivery, pelvic surgery, or in postmenopausal women; assess for pelvic floor weakness.
Positive cough stress test with a full bladder; leakage stops immediately when the provocation stops.
No significant urgency, frequency, or nocturia unless a mixed picture is present.
Sudden, compelling urge to void immediately followed by involuntary leakage, often before reaching the toilet.
Associated with urgency, frequency, and nocturia (overlaps with overactive bladder, but leakage volume is typically larger).
Not triggered by coughing, sneezing, or exertion alone; the urge precedes the leak.
May respond to antimuscarinics or beta-3 agonists (e.g., Mirabegron); rule out a urinary tract infection as a precipitant.
Continuous or frequent dribbling from an overdistended bladder, often with a sensation of incomplete emptying.
Usually secondary to bladder outlet obstruction (e.g., prostatic hyperplasia, urethral stricture) or an underactive/neurogenic detrusor.
Elevated post-void residual on bladder scan is the key distinguishing test.
Weak or intermittent stream and straining to void are common accompanying symptoms.
Urgency, usually with frequency and nocturia, with or without urgency incontinence, in the absence of infection or other clear pathology.
A symptom complex rather than a single mechanism; diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out infection, stones, and malignancy.
Improvement with antimuscarinics or beta-3 agonists (e.g., Mirabegron) supports the diagnosis.
No leakage specifically tied to coughing or sneezing, which helps distinguish it from pure stress incontinence.
Umbrella term covering storage symptoms (urgency, frequency, nocturia), voiding symptoms (weak stream, hesitancy, straining), and post-micturition symptoms (dribbling, incomplete emptying).
In men, frequently secondary to bladder outlet obstruction from prostatic hyperplasia; in women, often overlaps with overactive bladder or pelvic floor dysfunction.
A mixed symptom picture, both storage and voiding complaints together, favors this diagnosis over a single-mechanism condition.
Uroflowmetry and post-void residual help separate storage-predominant from voiding-predominant presentations.
Often asymptomatic early; may present with lower urinary tract symptoms, hematuria, or bone pain in advanced/metastatic disease.
Elevated or rapidly rising PSA; irregular, hard, or nodular prostate on digital rectal examination.
Distinguish from benign hyperplasia: hyperplasia typically has a smooth, symmetrically enlarged prostate and a PSA rise proportional to gland size.
Confirmed by prostate biopsy or MRI-targeted biopsy, not by symptoms alone.
Progressive voiding symptoms (weak stream, hesitancy, straining, incomplete emptying) in men typically over 50 years old.
Smooth, symmetrically enlarged prostate on digital rectal examination; PSA may be mildly elevated proportional to gland volume.
No hematuria, irregular or nodular gland, or rapid PSA rise, which would instead suggest prostate carcinoma.
Symptoms often improve with alpha-blockers or 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, supporting a benign obstructive process.
Voiding symptoms similar to prostatic hyperplasia but typically occurring after prior prostate or bladder neck surgery (e.g., TURP) or chronic inflammation.
Cystoscopy shows a narrowed, fibrotic bladder neck without significant prostatic enlargement.
History of pelvic or prostate surgery or radiation is a strong differentiating clue versus de novo hyperplasia.
Uroflow shows an obstructive pattern despite a small or previously resected prostate.
Progressive weak, split, or spraying urinary stream with straining, often with a history of urethritis, instrumentation, catheterization, or trauma.
Point tenderness or a palpable fibrotic segment along the urethra in some cases.
Retrograde urethrogram or cystoscopy demonstrates a discrete narrowed segment, distinguishing it from diffuse bladder outlet pathology like prostatic hyperplasia.
Post-void residual elevated; recurrent urinary tract infections may occur from stasis proximal to the stricture.
Voiding dysfunction (retention, overflow incontinence, or urge incontinence) occurring alongside a known neurological condition (spinal cord injury, diabetes, multiple sclerosis, stroke).
Loss of normal bladder sensation or coordination between the detrusor and sphincter (detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia) on urodynamics.
Absence of a structural outlet lesion (stricture, hyperplasia) on imaging or cystoscopy; the deficit is functional or neurological, not mechanical.
Saddle anesthesia, altered perineal sensation, or other neurological deficits support this diagnosis.
Painless gross or microscopic hematuria is the hallmark presenting symptom, often intermittent.
Irritative voiding symptoms (urgency, frequency, dysuria) can occur, especially with carcinoma in situ, mimicking cystitis.
Risk factors: smoking, occupational aromatic amine exposure, chronic cystitis or schistosomiasis, age over 50.
Cystoscopy with biopsy is diagnostic; imaging and cytology support but do not replace direct visualization.
Suprapubic or perineal pain that worsens with movement or at the end of micturition, sometimes with interruption of the urinary stream (positional voiding).
Hematuria (often terminal) and irritative voiding symptoms are common; may coexist with bladder outlet obstruction (e.g., prostatic hyperplasia) as a predisposing cause.
Plain X-ray, ultrasound, or CT demonstrates a radio-opaque or echogenic stone within the bladder.
Distinguish from bladder carcinoma by imaging (calcified, mobile stone versus a fixed soft-tissue mass) and cystoscopy.
Acute dysuria, frequency, urgency, and suprapubic discomfort, sometimes with hematuria; fever or flank pain suggest upper tract (pyelonephritis) involvement.
Positive urinalysis (leukocyte esterase, nitrites, pyuria) and a positive urine culture confirm the diagnosis.
Symptoms are typically acute in onset, unlike the chronic, progressive course of obstructive or malignant lower urinary tract symptoms.
Resolution of symptoms after appropriate antibiotics supports the diagnosis and helps exclude a structural or neoplastic cause.
Suprapubic pain or pressure, dysuria, urgency, and frequency, without significant flank pain or fever, which would suggest pyelonephritis.
Pyuria and bacteriuria on urinalysis or culture in bacterial cystitis; sterile pyuria may suggest interstitial cystitis or another cause.
Consider vesical calculus or a bladder tumor as an underlying cause if cystitis is recurrent or refractory to treatment.
Symptom overlap with urethritis: cystitis typically has more suprapubic and urgency symptoms, while urethritis is more discharge and urethral-pain predominant.
Vaginal discharge, odor, itching, or irritation, sometimes with external dysuria (pain as urine passes over inflamed labia rather than true internal dysuria).
Wet mount, vaginal pH, and culture identify the causative organism (e.g., bacterial vaginosis, candidiasis, trichomoniasis).
Absence of true urinary frequency or urgency and a negative urine culture help distinguish it from a primary urinary tract infection.
A relevant differential in women presenting with 'dysuria' where urinalysis is unremarkable.
Urethral discharge, itching, or burning with dysuria, often related to a sexually transmitted infection (e.g., Chlamydia, Gonorrhea).
Urethral swab or NAAT identifies the pathogen; urine culture may be negative despite symptoms, distinguishing it from cystitis or a typical urinary tract infection.
In men, consider prostatitis if symptoms include perineal pain or a tender prostate on exam; in women, consider vaginitis if discharge is vaginal rather than urethral.
A recent new sexual partner or unprotected intercourse is a relevant historical clue.
Perineal, suprapubic, or low back pain with dysuria, frequency, and sometimes fever or chills in acute bacterial prostatitis.
Tender, boggy, or warm prostate on digital rectal exam; avoid vigorous prostatic massage in suspected acute bacterial prostatitis.
Positive urine culture or expressed prostatic secretions support a bacterial etiology; chronic pelvic pain syndrome is a diagnosis of exclusion without infection.
Distinguish from hyperplasia or carcinoma by the acute or subacute onset of pain and infective signs rather than a purely progressive obstructive course.
